meta_title: Complete Guide to Window Cleaning in the Shuswap | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: Everything Shuswap homeowners need to know about window cleaning — frequency, seasonal timing, DIY vs pro, hard water, cost, and local considerations.
h1: The Complete Guide to Window Cleaning in the Shuswap
intro: This guide covers everything a Shuswap homeowner or property owner needs to know about keeping windows clean in a climate that presents unique challenges — mineral-rich lake spray, intense UV, cottonwood pollen, freeze-thaw grime cycles, and a compressed cleaning calendar. Whether you're deciding how often to clean, weighing DIY versus professional service, or trying to understand a hard water problem developing on your lakefront property, this guide gives you the full picture. It's written by Wagon Windows, a Salmon Arm-based service with direct experience across the full Shuswap region.

sections.h2: How Often Should You Clean Windows in the Shuswap?
sections.body:
The honest answer is: it depends on your property. The Shuswap is not a uniform environment. A home on a paved street in downtown Salmon Arm has different cleaning needs than a lakefront property in Blind Bay or a cabin near the Squilax-Anglemont Road.

For most full-time Salmon Arm residential properties, a twice-yearly schedule — spring and fall — is the practical baseline. Spring catches the winter residue (road salt, freeze-thaw mineral deposits, early cottonwood pollen) before UV locks it in. Fall removes the summer accumulation (dust, insect debris, UV-bonded film) and leaf tannin deposits before they freeze in place over winter. Two cleans per year keeps glass in good condition and prevents the gradual buildup that eventually requires harder intervention.

Lakefront properties on Shuswap Lake, Mara Lake, and Mabel Lake benefit from a third clean — a mid-summer pass in late June or July. These properties face continuous mineral deposit from lake spray, boat wake mist, and irrigation overspray. The calcium and magnesium content of Shuswap Lake water is high enough that, over a single summer season, significant haze can develop on south- and west-facing glass without a mid-season clean.

Vacation rental properties should be treated as commercial-grade frequency — a clean before the season opens, a mid-season touch-up, and a fall close-out is the recommended schedule. Guest experience is materially affected by window clarity, and listing photos are your primary marketing asset.

Properties in outlying communities — Enderby, Armstrong, Chase — can typically maintain a twice-yearly schedule unless they have specific exposure (gravel roads, agricultural dust, irrigation systems). Properties near orchard operations east of Salmon Arm pick up fine dust and spray during spring and summer that warrants a look at three cleans per year.

One practical rule: if you can see smearing, haze, or a visible film from inside the house in daylight, you're overdue.
bullets: Full-time urban residential: 2x per year (spring + fall)
Lakefront properties: 3x per year (spring, mid-summer, fall)
Vacation rentals: pre-season, mid-season, post-season
Gravel road / agricultural exposure: assess after 3 months

sections.h2: The Shuswap Cleaning Calendar — When to Clean and Why
sections.body:
The Shuswap's climate creates a predictable annual cycle that drives when window cleaning is most valuable. Understanding the calendar helps you time your cleans for maximum impact.

March–April: Pre-season for vacation properties and the opening window for residential spring cleans. Snow is off the ground by late March in Salmon Arm proper, though higher-elevation properties may still have ground frost. This is when seasonal property owners in Blind Bay, Sorrento, and Sicamous want pre-season service before May long weekend. Book early — this period books out fast.

April–May: Peak residential spring season. Cottonwood pollen peaks in April and creates a sticky film on all exterior glass. The spring clean should happen after cottonwood pollen has subsided — typically mid to late May is ideal for removing the full winter and pollen accumulation in one pass. For properties near the lake, spring wind events can deposit mineral-laden lake spray in March and April, adding urgency to a May clean.

June–July: The window for mid-season cleans, particularly for lakefront and vacation rental properties. UV intensity is highest from late June through August. A June or July clean removes accumulated dust and film before it is baked further onto the glass by peak summer heat.

September–October: Fall clean season. This is the last opportunity for exterior wet cleaning before freeze-up. Salmon Arm's first hard frost typically arrives in early to mid-October. The practical fall cleaning window is September through the third week of October. Beyond that, overnight temperatures create ice risk during cleaning and on freshly cleaned glass. October cleans should also include gutters — birch and cottonwood leaf-fall in the Shuswap runs through mid-October and fills gutters rapidly.

November–March: No exterior cleaning season. Interior-only work is possible and worthwhile, particularly for condensation haze on interior glass in older homes or properties with wood heat.

sections.h2: Types of Window Cleaning Service — What's Included and What Isn't
sections.body:
Not all window cleaning quotes cover the same work. Understanding what each service type includes helps you compare prices accurately and ensures you get what you actually want.

Exterior-only is the most common service and the baseline for most residential quotes. The cleaner works from the outside, cleaning all accessible exterior glass surfaces. This addresses the majority of visible dirt — road dust, pollen, lake spray, UV film — because exterior surfaces are the primary accumulation point. Exterior-only is the right choice for most spring and fall maintenance cleans.

Interior + exterior is a more comprehensive service. Interior glass accumulates condensation haze, fingerprints, pet nose prints, cooking splatter near kitchen windows, and mould spores in humid or older homes. The difference is striking if interiors haven't been done in 12+ months. Interior work also requires more time — the cleaner works from inside the home with protective floor covering and careful handling around furniture and window dressings. Interior + exterior is strongly recommended for pre-season vacation home opens and for any property that hasn't had a full clean in over a year.

Screen cleaning is almost always an add-on rather than included. Screens collect significant debris — cottonwood fuzz, spider webs, pollen, insect remains — and a dirty screen transfers material back to freshly cleaned glass in the first rain or wind event. We recommend screen cleaning whenever you're doing a full clean. Cost is $4 per screen.

Track and sill cleaning is another add-on. Tracks accumulate grit, dead insects, and oxidation from aluminum frames that grinds against seals and rollers over time. Clean tracks extend the life of your window hardware and prevent grit from contacting cleaned glass panes. This is particularly valuable in fall before windows are closed up for winter.

Hard water stain removal is a specialized service, separate from standard cleaning, covered in detail in our Hard Water Stain Removal guide. It involves targeted chemical treatment and mechanical work beyond standard cleaning methods.
bullets: Exterior-only: best value for maintenance cleans
Interior + exterior: full clean, recommended annually
Screen cleaning: $4/screen, strongly recommended with any full clean
Track/sill cleaning: from $65, recommended in fall
Hard water stain removal: quoted on-site after assessment

sections.h2: DIY vs Professional Window Cleaning — When It's Worth Calling In
sections.body:
DIY window cleaning is completely viable for ground-floor exterior windows on a single-storey home. A good squeegee, a bucket of soapy water, and an hour of effort will clean accessible windows adequately. The limitations appear quickly when you add height, scale, and complexity.

Ladder work is the first and most significant argument for professional service. Second-storey windows require working from a ladder with a bucket, squeegee, and wet hands — a combination that creates real fall risk. Professional window cleaners work from ladders routinely and are equipped and insured for it. For a two-storey home, the math on professional versus DIY shifts quickly when you factor in the time, ladder rental or purchase cost, and injury risk.

Result quality is the second consideration. Achieving a streak-free finish on large panes in variable light conditions is a skill. Common DIY problems include streaks from squeegee technique errors, missed edges, lint contamination from cotton cloths, and solution residue. Professional cleaners use commercial-grade squeegees, appropriate cleaning solutions, and technique developed over hundreds of jobs. The result on large or prominent windows is typically noticeably better.

Scale and time are the third factor. Cleaning a 20-window home thoroughly takes a homeowner most of a day. A professional crew completes the same job in 2–3 hours. For twice-yearly cleans, the time savings alone often justify the cost for busy households.

Where DIY makes sense: ground-floor windows in between professional cleans, spot cleaning after a dust storm or specific event, interior spot cleaning for fingerprints or pet marks. Where professional is clearly better: any second-storey work, full-property twice-yearly maintenance, hard water treatment, vacation rental turnarounds, and commercial properties.

sections.h2: Understanding Hard Water on Shuswap Windows
sections.body:
Hard water staining is one of the most common and most misunderstood window problems in the Shuswap. It's distinct from ordinary dirt and requires different treatment — a standard cleaning won't touch it once it's established.

The Shuswap region draws water from sources with relatively high mineral content — particularly calcium and magnesium. When this water contacts glass and evaporates (from lake spray, irrigation overspray, or even normal rain that picks up mineral particles), it leaves behind a thin mineral deposit. A single episode produces barely visible haze. Repeated over a season or multiple seasons, these deposits build into clearly visible white or grey haze, often appearing first on south- and west-facing glass that receives direct spray combined with intense sun.

Early-stage hard water deposits — one season or less — typically respond to professional cleaning with appropriate acidic cleaning solutions. The deposits haven't yet bonded firmly to the glass surface, and targeted cleaning removes them without damage.

Moderate hard water staining — two to four seasons of accumulated deposits — requires more intervention. At this stage, mineral deposits have formed a more established bond with the glass surface and may require buffing compounds combined with chemical treatment. This is still reversible but requires specialized equipment and materials beyond what standard cleaning provides.

Advanced hard water etching — five or more years of untreated deposits under UV exposure — may have caused physical damage to the glass surface itself. At this stage, the glass has been microscopically pitted, and the haze cannot be fully removed. Partial improvement is sometimes achievable, but full clarity restoration may not be possible. This is the important reason to address hard water deposits early.

Prevention is simpler than treatment: redirect irrigation systems away from glass, address drainage that routes mineral-laden water across windows, and maintain a regular cleaning schedule so deposits don't accumulate between cleans. See our dedicated Hard Water Stain Removal guide for full detail on assessment, treatment options, and prevention.

sections.h2: What Does Window Cleaning Cost in the Shuswap?
sections.body:
Window cleaning pricing in the Shuswap varies based on several factors: the number of windows, whether you want interior + exterior or exterior-only, home height (single vs. two-storey), add-ons like screens and tracks, and location relative to Salmon Arm.

For residential service, expect exterior-only pricing starting around $169 for a smaller home (up to 15 windows). A medium home (16–25 windows) exterior-only runs approximately $229. Interior + exterior packages add roughly $60–$80 to each tier. Large homes with 26–35 windows run from $299 exterior-only, from $369 interior + exterior.

Screens are an add-on at $4 per screen. Track cleaning is $4 per track. Hard water stain removal is quoted on-site after assessment — it varies significantly based on severity and the number of affected panes.

For gutter cleaning, Gutter cleaning starts from $189, assessed on-site.

For commercial properties, small storefronts start at $169. Medium commercial with 6–15 panes from $189. Recurring contract rates for weekly or bi-weekly service are negotiated directly.

A travel fee applies to all communities outside Salmon Arm — $35 for Blind Bay, Sorrento, Tappen, Chase, and nearby areas; $55 for Sicamous, Enderby, Armstrong, Eagle Bay, Celista, Scotch Creek, Grindrod, Spallumcheen, Swansea Point, and Anglemont. Window cleaning rates are otherwise consistent across the service area.

Wagon Windows publishes its residential pricing online at wagonwindow.com/pricing because we believe homeowners should be able to budget without a guessing game. We're happy to provide a free quote for any property based on a description or photos before booking.
bullets: Residential exterior-only from $169 (small home)
Residential interior + exterior from $219 (small home)
Gutter cleaning from $189 (assessed on-site)
Commercial storefronts from $169
Free quotes available — call (403) 837-5092

faqs.q: How long does a professional window clean take on a typical Salmon Arm home?
faqs.a: A medium-sized home — 16 to 25 windows — typically takes 2 to 3 hours for an exterior-only clean, or 3.5 to 5 hours for interior + exterior. Larger homes or those with many divided-light windows (multiple panes per frame) take longer. We'll give you a time estimate when booking based on your home's size and configuration.

faqs.q: Do I need to be home during the clean?
faqs.a: For exterior-only service, no — you don't need to be present as long as we have access to the exterior of the property and any gates or latches are unlocked. For interior + exterior service, someone needs to be home to grant access and be present while we work inside. We can coordinate access details in advance for vacation rental properties or homes managed by a property manager.

faqs.q: What if it rains right after my windows are cleaned?
faqs.a: Light rain shortly after a professional clean typically doesn't cause visible streaking — rain itself is mineral-free, and professionally cleaned glass sheds water cleanly. Problems arise when mineral-laden water (from gutters, overspray, or irrigation) contacts freshly cleaned glass and dries. Ordinary rain on clean glass is generally fine. If a significant rain event causes visible spotting within 24 hours of your clean, contact us and we'll assess.

faqs.q: Can you clean windows on older homes with aluminum frames without damaging them?
faqs.a: Yes. Aluminum-framed windows are common throughout Salmon Arm and the Shuswap, and we work with them routinely. We avoid abrasive materials on aluminum frames and use appropriate solutions that won't accelerate oxidation. If frames show existing oxidation, we'll note it — we don't hide pre-existing frame condition. If you want frames cleaned and polished as part of the service, let us know when booking.

faqs.q: Do you offer a satisfaction guarantee?
faqs.a: Yes. If you're not satisfied with the quality of the clean, contact us within 48 hours and we'll return to address any missed areas or quality issues at no additional charge. We stand behind our work, and if something doesn't meet standard, we want to know about it and fix it.

faqs.q: How do I get a quote for my home?
faqs.a: Call us at (403) 837-5092 or contact us through wagonwindow.com. For most homes, we can provide a clear pricing range based on a brief description of your home — number of windows, storeys, and whether you want exterior-only or interior + exterior. For larger homes, unusual configurations, or hard water assessment, an on-site look lets us quote accurately. Quotes are free and without obligation.

meta_title: Hard Water Stain Removal from Windows: Complete Guide | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: Shuswap hard water haze explained — causes, chemistry, stages of damage, DIY risks, professional treatment, and long-term prevention. Full guide by Wagon Windows.
h1: Hard Water Stain Removal from Windows: The Complete Guide
intro: Hard water staining is the most common and most misunderstood window problem in the Shuswap. It looks like dirt but doesn't clean like dirt. Left untreated, it progresses from annoying haze to permanent glass damage. This guide explains exactly what hard water staining is, how it progresses, what treatment options exist at each stage, the real risks of DIY removal, and what a professional treatment process looks like. It's written for Shuswap homeowners specifically, where the mineral content of lake water and irrigation systems makes hard water damage unusually prevalent.

sections.h2: What Causes Hard Water Stains on Windows?
sections.body:
Hard water staining begins with water — specifically, water that contains dissolved minerals, primarily calcium carbonate and magnesium. When this water contacts glass and then evaporates, it doesn't disappear cleanly. The minerals stay behind as a thin crystalline deposit on the glass surface.

In the Shuswap, the sources of this mineral-laden water are numerous. Shuswap Lake, Mara Lake, and Mabel Lake all carry dissolved minerals from their watershed geology — largely granite and limestone-influenced catchments in the Columbia Mountains. When wind or boat wake produces spray that reaches lakefront windows, each droplet that dries leaves a tiny mineral ring. Over a summer season of repeated events, these rings layer up.

Irrigation is a major second source, particularly on properties with older or poorly aimed sprinkler systems. Irrigation water in the South Shuswap and Salmon Arm area is drawn largely from surface sources with moderate to high mineral content. Even a single season of a sprinkler head grazing a window will produce visible mineral banding at the spray line.

Rainwater itself is relatively mineral-free, but rain that runs off mineral-laden surfaces — stucco, concrete, brick — and drains down across glass picks up those minerals in transit. Properties with overhanging eaves that direct mineral-rich runoff across window glass are particularly vulnerable. This is an underappreciated source: the clean-looking water running down the face of your home after rain can carry more dissolved mineral than you'd expect.

The chemistry is straightforward: calcium carbonate deposits form a crystalline structure on glass, and this structure has significant adhesion to the silica surface of glass. Early deposits are physically loosely bonded. Under UV exposure and repeated wetting and drying cycles, the bond strengthens. This is why time is the enemy — early intervention is always easier and cheaper than late-stage treatment.
bullets: Lake spray (Shuswap Lake, Mara Lake, Mabel Lake)
Irrigation overspray — the most preventable source
Mineral-rich runoff from stucco, concrete, and brick
Groundwater sprinkler systems with high mineral content

sections.h2: The Chemistry of Hard Water Damage — Why It's Not Just Dirt
sections.body:
Understanding why hard water staining behaves differently from ordinary dirt explains why standard cleaning doesn't work and why the wrong DIY approach can make things worse.

Ordinary window dirt — dust, pollen, organic matter, insect residue — is not chemically bonded to glass. It sits on the surface, held by surface tension and static charge. Soap and water break this bond easily. A squeegee removes the loosened material cleanly.

Calcium carbonate deposits are different. They form a crystalline lattice on the glass surface that has actual chemical affinity for silica, the main component of glass. The deposit is not merely sitting on the surface — it is partially adhered to it. The longer it remains and the more UV exposure it receives, the stronger that adhesion becomes.

The common DIY instinct — scrubbing harder — makes things worse at the intermediate and advanced stages. Abrasive scrubbing can scratch the glass surface, creating micro-scratches that make future staining adhere even more readily and scatter light, making the glass appear permanently cloudy. This is the primary hard water DIY injury we encounter: homeowners who have scratched their glass using abrasive pads trying to remove mineral deposits.

Acid is the correct chemical approach, because calcium carbonate is alkaline and dissolves in acid. Weak acid solutions — white vinegar is the accessible DIY option — work on fresh, light deposits. But vinegar's acidity (approximately 5% acetic acid) is insufficient for moderate to advanced deposits, and prolonged application of any acid to window seals, frames, and rubber gaskets causes deterioration. Professional treatment uses stronger, formulated acid cleaners at appropriate concentrations with careful frame protection.

The irreversibility point is important to understand: once calcium deposits have created physical pitting in the glass surface through long-term UV exposure and crystal growth, that pitting cannot be removed with chemical treatment. Polishing compounds can minimize the appearance of mild pitting, but the glass surface has been permanently altered. This is the point of no return, and it is reached faster than most homeowners realize — often within three to five years of consistent untreated exposure.

sections.h2: Stages of Hard Water Damage — How to Assess What You're Looking At
sections.body:
Hard water staining progresses through distinct stages with different treatment implications. Correctly identifying the stage helps you understand what's achievable and what the treatment will involve.

Stage 1 — Fresh deposit (0–12 months): The mineral layer is thin and loosely bonded. Glass appears lightly hazy or shows faint white spotting, most visible at certain angles in direct light. At this stage, professional cleaning with appropriate cleaning solutions will typically remove the deposit fully. No specialized stain removal treatment is required. Regular cleaning prevents return.

Stage 2 — Established deposit (1–3 years): Multiple seasons of mineral accumulation have built a more substantial layer. Haze is clearly visible from inside the home in daylight. The deposit does not respond to standard cleaning products. Professional treatment with targeted acidic cleaners and appropriate dwell time removes most of the deposit. Some mild residual haziness may remain on glass with partial pitting. This stage is the most common presentation we encounter on Shuswap properties.

Stage 3 — Entrenched deposit with early surface etching (3–5 years): The deposit has been repeatedly wetted and dried under UV exposure, and the crystalline structure has begun to physically stress the glass surface. Haze is heavy and clearly visible even in diffuse light. Professional acid treatment combined with polishing compounds is required. Full clarity restoration may not be possible — realistic outcome is significant improvement, not guaranteed return to original clarity. Treatment cost is higher due to time and materials required.

Stage 4 — Advanced etching (5+ years): Physical damage to the glass surface has occurred. The silica matrix has been microscopically altered. Haze may have a three-dimensional, frosted quality that doesn't change at different viewing angles. At this stage, chemical treatment has limited effect because the haziness is in the glass, not on it. Window replacement becomes the practical solution for full clarity restoration. Professional assessment is needed to determine whether treatment or replacement is the more cost-effective path.

On-site assessment is the only reliable way to determine stage. We assess every hard water project before quoting.
bullets: Stage 1 (fresh): Responds to professional cleaning — full removal expected
Stage 2 (established): Acid treatment required — high likelihood of full removal
Stage 3 (early etching): Acid + polish — significant improvement, full clarity not guaranteed
Stage 4 (advanced): Surface damage — treatment limited, replacement may be necessary

sections.h2: DIY Hard Water Stain Removal — What Works, What Doesn't, and What Causes Damage
sections.body:
DIY hard water removal is worth attempting on Stage 1 deposits. It becomes progressively less effective and more risky as severity increases. Understanding the boundaries helps you avoid the common damage scenarios.

White vinegar works for fresh, light deposits. Apply undiluted to the affected area, allow 5–10 minutes of dwell time, and scrub gently with a non-abrasive applicator (a sponge or soft-bristle brush, not steel wool or an abrasive pad). Rinse thoroughly. This works because fresh calcium carbonate deposits are alkaline and respond to acetic acid. The limitation is concentration — at 5% acetic acid, vinegar can only dissolve light deposits. More applications do not substitute for stronger acid on established deposits.

Citric acid solutions (lemon juice or commercial citric acid powder dissolved in water) are marginally more effective than vinegar due to slightly higher acidity and better wetting properties. They carry the same limitations on moderate to heavy deposits.

The most common DIY mistakes: using abrasive scrub pads (green scotch-brite, steel wool, abrasive stone), which scratch glass irreversibly; applying acid cleaners to rubber seals and frames without protection, causing seal degradation; using razor blades on glass that has even mild surface etching, which deepens scratches; and applying more product more often in place of actually effective treatment, allowing the deposit to continue bonding while you wait.

Razor blade scraping on clean, unscratched glass by an experienced person is a legitimate technique for removing certain deposits — glass is harder than most contaminants, and a sharp blade at the correct angle removes deposits without scratching. However, this requires a perfectly sharp blade, correct technique, and wet glass. A dull blade, dry glass, or the wrong angle produces scratches. We do not recommend razor blade use for homeowners on hard water staining.

When to stop and call a professional: if the deposit doesn't move meaningfully after two correctly executed DIY attempts, or if you're looking at anything that appears to be Stage 2 or above, professional treatment is the right call. The cost of a professional hard water treatment is considerably lower than replacing scratched glass.

sections.h2: The Professional Hard Water Treatment Process
sections.body:
Professional hard water stain removal differs from standard window cleaning in both the products used and the process. Here's what a professional treatment looks like when done correctly.

Assessment first. Before any treatment begins, the severity of the deposit is assessed. Stage determination affects both the products used and the realistic outcome. We always communicate the assessment and expected result before starting — there should be no surprises at the end of a hard water treatment job.

Frame and seal protection. Acid-based cleaners are effective on calcium carbonate but aggressive on rubber seals, anodized aluminum frames, and painted surfaces. Proper masking or careful application technique protects these components. This step is often skipped in less careful work, resulting in bleached or degraded seals that then require replacement.

Initial cleaning pass. Standard cleaning first removes any surface contamination — dust, pollen, organic matter — that would otherwise interfere with the acid treatment's contact with the deposit.

Acid application. Professional-grade acidic cleaners at appropriate concentration are applied to the deposit. Dwell time is calibrated to the severity. The cleaner works by dissolving the calcium carbonate structure, breaking its bond with the glass surface. Multiple applications with agitation are used on more established deposits.

Mechanical agitation. For Stage 2 and above, chemical treatment alone is usually insufficient. Buffing compounds and appropriate applicators are used to physically work the loosened deposit off the glass surface. This is the step where experience matters most — too aggressive and you create new scratches; too light and the deposit remains.

Final clean and rinse. Once the treatment is complete, the glass is cleaned with standard solution to remove treatment residue and buffing compound, then squeegeed clean. The result is assessed in appropriate light before the job is considered complete.

Prevention consultation. Before leaving, we identify the source of the hard water exposure where visible (irrigation, drainage, runoff) and make recommendations to prevent rapid re-staining.

sections.h2: Preventing Hard Water Stains — Long-Term Protection for Shuswap Windows
sections.body:
Prevention is straightforwardly more cost-effective than treatment. The measures that prevent hard water staining on Shuswap windows fall into three categories: source control, maintenance frequency, and protective coatings.

Source control is the most impactful. If you have irrigation systems, ensure all heads are aimed away from glass surfaces — even misting overspray is sufficient to cause staining over a season. Irrigation is the most preventable source of hard water damage we encounter. Adjust, cap, or relocate any head that routinely wets exterior glass. For properties with mineral-laden gutter drainage that runs down window surfaces, gutter extensions or downspout extensions can redirect the flow. Properties with stucco or concrete near window openings should check that rain runoff from these surfaces isn't channelled across glass.

Maintenance frequency is the second lever. Regular cleaning prevents the accumulation that allows Stage 1 deposits to progress to Stage 2. A property that is cleaned twice a year will rarely develop established hard water staining unless the exposure source is severe. The cleaning itself mechanically removes fresh deposits before they bond. This is the core reason we recommend lakefront properties on Shuswap Lake and Mara Lake maintain a three-clean-per-year schedule — the exposure is continuous enough that twice-yearly cleaning allows deposits to advance between cleans.

Protective glass coatings (hydrophobic coatings, sometimes called nano-coatings) are a growing category of prevention product. These coatings create a surface that causes water to bead and run off rather than spread and evaporate, significantly reducing mineral deposit formation. Professional-grade coatings applied after a fresh hard water treatment extend the interval before re-treatment is needed. Results vary by product and application quality. We can discuss coating options at the time of treatment for properties with ongoing exposure challenges.

The simple summary: fix your irrigation, clean regularly, and address any staining you notice within its first season. These three practices will prevent the vast majority of hard water glass damage.
bullets: Redirect any irrigation that contacts window glass — most preventable source
Maintain regular cleaning schedule — removes fresh deposits before they bond
Consider hydrophobic coatings on high-exposure properties
Address staining in its first season — Stage 1 treatment costs a fraction of Stage 3

faqs.q: Can hard water stains on my windows be removed permanently?
faqs.a: Stage 1 and most Stage 2 deposits can be fully removed, restoring original glass clarity. Stage 3 deposits can be significantly improved but may show residual haze where surface etching has occurred. Stage 4 deposits with deep surface pitting cannot be fully reversed by treatment — glass replacement becomes the practical path for full clarity. The key is addressing staining early, before it advances to the stages where removal is only partial.

faqs.q: How do I know if my white window haze is hard water or something else?
faqs.a: Hard water haze is most common on glass that receives spray or runoff from a mineral water source. It appears as white or grey clouding, often with faint ring patterns (dried droplet rings) visible on close inspection. It does not wipe off with a damp cloth. Ordinary dirt and pollen haze does respond to damp wiping. Oxidation from aluminum frames produces a grey powder on the glass edges near the frame. If you're unsure, contact us — we assess at no charge as part of any quote.

faqs.q: Does homeowner's insurance cover hard water damage to windows?
faqs.a: Generally, no. Hard water damage is classified as gradual deterioration or maintenance failure rather than sudden accidental damage, and most homeowner's policies exclude gradual damage. If the staining was caused by a neighbour's irrigation system or a building defect that caused abnormal drainage, there may be liability considerations — consult your broker. For most situations, hard water treatment is an out-of-pocket maintenance cost.

faqs.q: How long does a professional hard water treatment take?
faqs.a: It depends on the severity and number of panes affected. Stage 1–2 treatment on a typical residential property (8–12 affected windows) typically takes 3–5 hours, including assessment, treatment, and final clean. Stage 3 treatment takes longer due to multiple chemical application and buffing passes — often 5–8 hours on a medium-sized home. We provide a time estimate when we assess the job.

faqs.q: How soon after treatment can the windows get wet again?
faqs.a: Standard cleaning allows normal weather exposure immediately. If a hydrophobic coating is applied after treatment, there's typically a cure window of 24–48 hours where the coating should stay dry to bond properly. We'll specify any post-treatment precautions at the time of service.

faqs.q: If I've already scratched my windows trying to remove hard water stains, is it too late for professional treatment?
faqs.a: Not necessarily. Light surface scratches from DIY attempts can sometimes be minimized with professional polishing compound, and the underlying hard water deposit can still be treated. The scratches cannot be fully removed, but in many cases the visual improvement from treatment is significant even on scratched glass. We'll assess honestly — if the scratching is severe enough that treatment won't produce a meaningful improvement, we'll tell you before charging for the work.

meta_title: Gutter Cleaning Guide for BC Homeowners | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: The complete gutter cleaning guide for Shuswap & BC homeowners. Why gutters matter, Shuswap debris types, how to clean, DIY vs pro, frequency & cost.
h1: The Complete Gutter Cleaning Guide for BC Homeowners
intro: Gutters are the part of your home's exterior most homeowners think about least — until they fail. A blocked gutter in the Shuswap's wet-snow autumn or spring melt can send water into a fascia board, a soffit, or a foundation in a single season. This guide explains why gutters matter in the BC Interior specifically, what fills them in the Shuswap, the signs that yours need attention, what a proper clean involves, how to approach it as a DIY project safely, and what professional service costs. It's written from direct experience cleaning gutters across Salmon Arm, Blind Bay, Sicamous, Chase, Enderby, and Armstrong.

sections.h2: Why Gutters Matter — The Damage That Happens When They're Blocked
sections.body:
A functioning gutter system does one thing: moves water from your roof surface to a controlled discharge point away from your foundation. When gutters are blocked, they fail at this job in two distinct ways — overflow and weight — both of which cause significant damage.

Overflow damage occurs when a blocked gutter fills to capacity and water spills over the edge. In most homes, this means water running down the exterior wall adjacent to the gutter, over the fascia board, and into the soffit. Fascia boards are typically wood or composite and absorb water readily when repeatedly soaked — rot is the predictable outcome over one to two seasons. Soffit damage allows moisture into the roof structure and attic, where it creates conditions for mould and wood decay that can compromise structural components. In severe or prolonged cases, overflow water penetrates the wall assembly and reaches interior finishes.

Foundation damage is the second failure mode, and often the more expensive one. When water isn't carried to a discharge point away from the foundation, it pools against the foundation wall. In freeze-thaw climates like the Shuswap, water that penetrates foundation cracks freezes and expands, widening those cracks. Over multiple seasons, this process causes structural movement in foundations that is expensive to repair. A properly functioning gutter system with appropriate downspout extensions is one of the simplest foundation protection measures available.

Ice dam formation is the third failure mode specific to cold climates. Blocked gutters in autumn retain debris that holds moisture through winter. When roof snow melts and refreezes in a clogged gutter, the ice mass grows into the roof edge and can work under shingles, causing leaks that appear inside the home during warm spells in January or February — often the first sign homeowners recognize of a gutter problem.

All three of these damage modes are preventable by maintaining clear gutters. The cost of cleaning is trivially small compared to the cost of the damage it prevents.

sections.h2: What Fills Shuswap Gutters — Debris Types and Timing
sections.body:
The Shuswap's tree ecology creates a predictable annual pattern of gutter debris that differs from coastal BC or Alberta in ways worth understanding.

Birch trees are the primary autumn debris source across most of Salmon Arm and the Shuswap. Birch leaves are small, thin, and numerous — they compact into a dense mat in gutters that is far more resistant to flushing than larger leaves. Birch leaf fall peaks in October, and a birch-heavy property can have gutters packed full within two to three weeks of peak fall. Paper birch bark shreds also accumulate and mat similarly.

Cottonwood and trembling aspen are widespread in lower-elevation and riparian areas, particularly near the Salmon River and along lakeshores. Cottonwood leaf fall is dramatic and rapid — leaves fall mostly in a one to two week window in mid-October and can fill gutters in a single event. Cottonwood debris also includes significant catkin and seed material in spring and early summer, which creates a second annual loading event that many homeowners miss. Spring catkin flush in May is worth a gutter check.

Conifer needles — from ponderosa pine (abundant on south-facing slopes around Salmon Arm) and Douglas fir — are a slower but continuous source. Needle accumulation doesn't produce a single large fall event but builds steadily through the year. Needle mats in gutters hold water and support moss growth, both of which accelerate gutter deterioration.

Moss and lichen growth on roof surfaces produces material that washes into gutters during spring and fall rain events. Properties with north-facing roof sections and significant tree shading are particularly prone to roof moss, which should be addressed at the roof level to reduce ongoing gutter loading.

Gravel roof ballast from older flat or low-slope sections of commercial buildings, and roof granules from aging asphalt shingles on residential properties, accumulate in gutters and downspouts and contribute to blockages at elbow joints and downspout openings.
bullets: Birch leaves: October peak — compact, mat-forming, most common blockage cause
Cottonwood: mid-October leaves + May catkins — two events per year
Conifer needles: continuous, slower loading — check north-facing sections
Moss and roof granules: wash in during rain events, block downspout inlets

sections.h2: Signs Your Gutters Need Cleaning
sections.body:
Gutters don't usually announce problems clearly — by the time visible damage appears, the cleaning interval was overdue by a season or more. These are the observable signs that indicate gutters are loaded or blocked.

Water overflowing the gutter edge during rain is the most obvious sign. If you see water sheeting over the front lip of your gutter during any rainfall heavier than light drizzle, the gutter is either blocked, sagging, or both. Overflow during only heavy rain may indicate partial blockage — the gutter can handle low flow but not peak flow.

Plant growth in gutters is a clear indicator of long-overdue cleaning. When debris has been sitting long enough to accumulate soil and moisture, seeds germinate and take root. Visible green growth from a gutter — grasses, small shrubs, tree seedlings — means the debris has been there for at least one growing season, likely longer.

Sagging gutter sections occur when accumulated debris holds water and the combined weight exceeds what the hangers can support. A gutter section pulling away from the fascia or visibly drooping at its midpoint indicates either overloaded hangers or rotted fascia behind the hanger — both warrant prompt attention.

Water staining on the fascia board or exterior siding below gutter lines indicates overflow that has been occurring for some time. Look for discoloration, paint peeling, or wood darkening along the wall directly below the gutter run.

Basement moisture in late autumn or spring, without another clear source, should prompt a gutter inspection. The connection between gutter overflow and foundation moisture isn't always obvious because the water travels down the wall and into the soil before reaching the foundation, but it's a common pathway.

Standing water in the gutter visible from a window or upper-level vantage point indicates a level or sagging gutter section that isn't draining, even if it's not yet blocked with debris.
bullets: Water overflowing gutter edge during rain
Visible plant growth from inside the gutter
Sagging gutter sections or hangers pulling away from fascia
Water staining on fascia or siding below the gutter line
Unexplained basement moisture in autumn or spring

sections.h2: What a Professional Gutter Clean Includes
sections.body:
A professional gutter clean is more than scooping debris. Understanding what the service includes helps you evaluate what you're getting and ask the right questions when booking.

Debris removal is the core task — removing all leaves, needles, seed pods, dirt, and accumulated organic matter from the gutter channel. This is done by hand or with a tool working from a ladder, not by blowing debris around with a leaf blower (which leaves fine particles behind and creates a mess on your property). The debris is bagged and removed from the property or composted on-site depending on the job.

Downspout clearing is equally important. Many gutter blockages occur at the top inlet of the downspout, where debris accumulates at the throat, or at elbow joints where the pipe changes direction. A complete clean includes clearing downspouts from the top and confirming flow through to the discharge point. We flush downspouts with water where necessary to confirm they're fully clear — a downspout that seems open may have a partial blockage that the next major rainfall will complete.

Gutter flushing with water is the final step in a thorough clean. After debris removal, flushing the gutter channel confirms that the pitch is correct (water flows to the downspout, not toward the centre of the run or back toward the roofline) and surfaces any remaining fine debris. Gutters that were installed with incorrect pitch may collect water in the middle of a run even after cleaning — flagging this during a clean is valuable information for the homeowner.

Condition report. Professional service should include notes on any concerning observations: cracked seams, loose hangers, evidence of fascia rot behind the gutter, missing end caps, or downspout disconnections. These observations cost nothing to report and may prevent a much more expensive repair if caught early.

For fall cleans specifically, downspout extension confirmation is important — ensuring extensions are in place and directing water away from the foundation before winter, when adjusting them is more difficult.
bullets: Full debris removal — by hand, not blown into the yard
Downspout clearing and flush-through confirmation
Gutter channel flush to check pitch and residual debris
Condition report on seams, hangers, and fascia observations

sections.h2: DIY Gutter Cleaning — How to Do It Safely
sections.body:
DIY gutter cleaning is a legitimate approach for single-storey homes with relatively accessible gutters. The primary risk is ladder falls, and this risk is not trivial — gutter cleaning is one of the most common causes of DIY ladder injuries because it requires repeated ladder moves along a run, often with full hands.

Ladder safety is the non-negotiable first requirement. Use a ladder rated for your weight plus tools and debris. Do not lean the ladder against the gutter itself — a gutter is not a structural support and will bend or detach under lateral load. Use a ladder standoff or stay to keep the ladder away from the gutter and resting on the wall instead. Never lean sideways past your hips to reach further along the gutter — move the ladder instead. Have a second person present to foot the ladder and hand materials up.

For single-storey homes, extension ladders or sturdy stepladders work adequately. For two-storey homes, the working height increases the fall hazard significantly. A fall from second-storey height is potentially fatal. This is the primary reason many homeowners who are comfortable DIYing single-storey gutters choose to hire out two-storey work.

The cleaning process: work from one end of the run toward the downspout, removing debris into a bucket clipped to the ladder. Start at the end farthest from the downspout and work toward it, so loosened debris doesn't block the throat you're working toward. Wear gloves — gutter debris is often a combination of decomposed organic matter, insect remains, and sharp material from roof granules.

After debris removal, flush the gutter with a garden hose to confirm pitch and clear the downspout. If the downspout doesn't clear with direct water pressure, a plumber's snake (drain snake) pushed down from the top will usually dislodge compacted blockages at elbow joints.

When to stop: if your gutters are on a two-storey or complex roofline, if the debris is compacted and heavy (significantly increasing load on the gutter hangers during removal), or if you observe any concerning structural issues during the clean — stop and call a professional.

sections.h2: How Often Should Gutters Be Cleaned in the Shuswap? And What Does It Cost?
sections.body:
Frequency recommendations for the Shuswap are more aggressive than the often-cited national average of once or twice yearly, because the Shuswap's tree ecology produces more gutter loading events per year than most regions.

For most Salmon Arm residential properties with significant tree coverage: twice yearly is the baseline — once in late October or early November after peak birch and cottonwood fall, and once in May or June to clear spring debris and the cottonwood catkin load. Properties with minimal tree coverage or predominantly paved surroundings may be able to maintain once yearly in fall only.

For properties with heavy conifer coverage: a third clean in late summer (August) to clear needle accumulation before fall loading is worth considering. Conifer needles hold moisture and support moss growth; removing them in late summer prevents the needle mat from compounding the fall leaf load into a single large blockage event.

For vacation properties closed through the winter: a pre-season spring clean (March or April) before opening should include a gutter check and flush, even if a fall clean was done before closing. Winter can bring down significant debris from conifers during snow and wind events, and accumulated debris through a Shuswap winter should be cleared before spring rain season.

Pricing in the Shuswap: starts from $189, assessed on-site. Complex rooflines, large homes, or properties with particularly heavy debris loading are quoted on-site. A gutter clean bundled with a window clean in fall typically results in efficient scheduling since we're already on-site with ladders.

For comparison to DIY cost: ladder rental is approximately $60–$90/day. Bucket, gloves, and tools add another $20–$40. Time on a medium-sized home is 3–5 hours for an inexperienced person. Professional service for the same home is completed in 1–2 hours. For twice-yearly service, the economics of professional cleaning are compelling even setting aside the ladder safety considerations.
bullets: Heavy tree coverage: twice yearly (October/November + May/June)
Light tree coverage / minimal debris: once yearly in fall
Heavy conifer coverage: add a third clean in August
Vacation properties: check and flush at seasonal opening

faqs.q: How do I know if my downspouts are blocked versus my gutter channel?
faqs.a: Pour water directly into the gutter channel near the downspout inlet while watching the downspout discharge. If water exits the downspout promptly, the downspout is clear and the blockage is in the channel. If water backs up at the downspout inlet or flows very slowly, the blockage is in the downspout itself — likely at an elbow joint or compacted debris at the throat. Most downspout blockages can be cleared with water pressure from the top or a drain snake.

faqs.q: Do gutter guards actually work? Are they worth it?
faqs.a: Gutter guards reduce the frequency of cleaning required but do not eliminate it. In the Shuswap specifically, fine debris — birch seeds, cottonwood fuzz, conifer needle fragments — bypasses most guard designs. Guards with fine mesh perform better than open-screen designs but still require annual cleaning. Our general view: guards are worth considering on properties with moderate debris load if the homeowner wants to reduce cleaning frequency; they're less effective on heavy birch or conifer properties and shouldn't be seen as a full solution.

faqs.q: My gutters are sagging. Is that a cleaning issue or a repair?
faqs.a: Sagging can result from accumulated weight (a cleaning issue — once debris is removed and the gutter dries out, lightweight gutters may partially recover) or from failed hangers and fascia rot (a repair issue). During a professional clean, we check hanger condition and flag any fascia deterioration we observe. If hangers need re-fastening or fascia repair is required, we'll note it clearly. Gutter repair itself is outside our scope — we'll recommend a contractor if structural work is needed.

faqs.q: When is the best single time of year to clean gutters in Salmon Arm if I'm only doing one clean per year?
faqs.a: Late October to early November — after peak leaf-fall, before the first significant freeze. This timing removes the full fall debris load (birch, cottonwood, early conifer accumulation) before winter freeze-up, ensures downspouts are clear before snow-melt events, and gives your gutters the best chance of functioning through winter without ice dam formation. If you can only do one clean per year, make it this one.

faqs.q: Can you clean gutters in winter or early spring when there's still snow on the roof?
faqs.a: We don't work on rooflines with significant snow or ice coverage — it creates unsafe working conditions for ladder use. Early spring gutter cleaning is possible once roof snow has cleared and overnight temperatures are above freezing consistently, which in Salmon Arm is typically mid-March to early April depending on the year. If you've missed the fall window, contact us in late February and we'll schedule you for the first available early-spring slot.

meta_title: Commercial Window Cleaning Guide for Shuswap Businesses | Wagon Windows
meta_desc: Everything Shuswap business owners need to know about commercial window cleaning — frequency, what's included, contracts vs one-off, cost & finding the right service.
h1: Commercial Window Cleaning Guide for Shuswap Businesses
intro: Clean windows are a silent part of how a business presents to its community. Customers form first impressions of a storefront before they walk through the door, and window clarity is a significant part of that impression — especially in a small-town market like Salmon Arm where reputation compounds locally. This guide covers what commercial window cleaning involves, how frequency should vary by business type, the difference between contract and one-off service, what a proper commercial clean costs in the Shuswap, and how to evaluate a service provider. It's written by Wagon Windows, which provides commercial cleaning across Salmon Arm and the Shuswap region.

sections.h2: Why Commercial Windows Matter — First Impressions in a Small Market
sections.body:
In a market the size of Salmon Arm, most customers make their decisions with some pre-existing awareness of a business. They've driven by your storefront, seen your sign, noticed your windows. Clean windows signal care and professionalism in ways that are felt before they're consciously registered. Dirty, streaked, or hazy windows communicate the opposite — that maintenance isn't a priority.

The research on retail psychology is consistent: customers are more likely to enter a store, and more likely to feel positive about their experience, in a well-maintained physical environment. Window clarity is a significant component of exterior presentation. This matters more in a small market than a large one because alternative options are limited — customers who don't enter because of a poor exterior impression are choosing a competitor, not a different location of the same chain.

Restaurants and food service businesses face additional scrutiny. Customers making decisions about where to eat are making implicit judgments about cleanliness and standards from the outside in. Dirty windows on a restaurant communicate something specific and negative about hygiene culture. In Salmon Arm's competitive food service market, exterior presentation is a meaningful differentiator.

Accommodations — particularly the motels, inns, and B&Bs along Highway 1 and on Lakeshore Drive — compete largely on visual presentation to drive-by and online traffic. Listing photos and street view impressions are the first filter. Clean windows are the fastest visual upgrade a property can make.

For professional offices — medical clinics, law firms, financial advisors — window clarity signals attention to detail and seriousness. Patients and clients entering a space with grimy windows have a subtly diminished confidence in the standards of the business overall, even if they can't articulate why.

sections.h2: How Often Should Commercial Windows Be Cleaned? By Business Type
sections.body:
Frequency should be calibrated to the business's foot traffic, visibility, and exposure to soiling factors. A one-size approach leads either to over-expenditure or to maintenance gaps that allow visible degradation.

High-foot-traffic retail (grocery, pharmacy, hardware, clothing stores): Windows should be cleaned every 2–4 weeks. High foot traffic near glass creates fingerprint and smudge accumulation on lower panes and entrance glass. The most visible panels — those framing the entrance and adjacent to the main display window — may warrant weekly attention. The Salmon Arm commercial strip on Trans-Canada Highway gets road spray from passing vehicles that adds an exterior soiling factor on top of foot traffic soil.

Food service (restaurants, cafes, bakeries): Every 2–4 weeks as a minimum. Interior cooking environments produce grease mist and condensation that affects interior glass. Exterior exposure varies by location. Street-facing windows that receive direct road spray or are adjacent to parking areas accumulate vehicle exhaust and dust faster than setback locations.

Accommodations (motels, inns, B&Bs): Monthly minimum during the Shuswap tourist season (May through September), quarterly off-season. Accommodations on Lakeshore Drive or with lake-facing rooms benefit from more frequent cleaning due to mineral deposit from lake spray and the expectations of summer guests.

Professional offices (medical, legal, financial, real estate): Every 4–8 weeks is appropriate. Soiling rates are lower than high-traffic retail, but the standard expected by clients is high. Most professional offices do well with bi-monthly service.

Light industrial and service businesses (auto shops, repair services, light manufacturing): Monthly to quarterly, depending on exterior exposure. Dust and exhaust from operations often accelerates soiling on these properties.

One-time or irregular commercial cleaning is appropriate for businesses with low glass area, limited customer-facing glass, or very low foot traffic environments.
bullets: High-traffic retail: every 2–4 weeks
Restaurants and food service: every 2–4 weeks
Accommodations: monthly in season, quarterly off-season
Professional offices: every 4–8 weeks
Light industrial / service: monthly to quarterly

sections.h2: What a Commercial Window Clean Includes
sections.body:
Commercial cleaning differs from residential in scope and scheduling requirements, not in the fundamental process. Understanding what is and isn't typically included helps avoid misaligned expectations.

Storefront glass — the primary display windows and entrance glass — is the core of any commercial clean. This is what customers see and what most directly affects first impressions. A thorough commercial clean uses commercial-grade squeegees sized appropriately for large commercial panes and professional solutions that minimize streak risk on wide glass spans.

Entrance glass and doors receive special attention in a good commercial clean. Door glass accumulates fingerprints and push/pull marks at handle height rapidly. On high-traffic storefronts, entrance glass may need more frequent spot cleaning than the main display windows. Some commercial clients opt for entrance-glass-only maintenance visits between full cleans.

Interior surfaces. Depending on the service configuration, interior glass may or may not be included. Retail environments with display merchandise close to glass need interior cleaning carefully coordinated with staff. We work around fixtures, merchandise, and staff schedules. Interior commercial cleaning requires more communication and coordination than residential and is typically scheduled during off-hours, early mornings, or before opening.

Signage and transoms. Glass above storefronts and decorative glass elements above entrance heights are often overlooked in basic cleans. We include these when accessible without specialized equipment.

High-access and multi-storey commercial work — anything requiring reach pole extension beyond standard or scaffolding access — is quoted separately. Most Salmon Arm commercial storefronts are single-storey and fully accessible from the ground with standard equipment.

Scheduling flexibility is a major consideration for commercial clients. We work early mornings (before business opening) or after hours for clients where operational disruption is a concern. For recurring contract clients, we establish a fixed schedule so cleaning days are predictable.

sections.h2: Contract vs One-Off Commercial Cleaning — What Makes Sense
sections.body:
The decision between a recurring contract and one-off bookings is primarily a question of how much variability a business can tolerate in its exterior presentation and how much scheduling overhead they want to manage.

Recurring contracts offer a fixed schedule, locked pricing, and reliability. The practical advantages are significant: the business doesn't have to think about when windows were last cleaned, doesn't have to rebook each time, and typically locks in pricing that is more favorable than one-off rates. For high-foot-traffic retail and food service businesses where consistent presentation matters, a contract that defines cleaning frequency and removes it from the weekly decision load is almost always the right choice.

Contracts also enable a service provider to optimize routing efficiently — when multiple clients in the same area are on the same schedule, the provider can batch visits and pass efficiency back to clients in the form of better pricing. This is particularly relevant in Salmon Arm where commercial properties along the Trans-Canada corridor can be serviced efficiently in sequence.

One-off or irregular service makes sense for businesses with seasonal presence, lower presentation standards requirements, or large variation in cleaning need. A business that is open primarily in summer and closed in winter has different needs than a year-round retailer. Similarly, a business whose customer base is entirely phone-based or delivery-based and who has minimal customer-facing glass may prefer to book one-off cleans when the windows become visibly problematic rather than maintaining a schedule.

The cost structure typically reflects the commitment: contract clients at weekly or bi-weekly frequency receive locked rates that are meaningfully lower per visit than one-off pricing. For a business that intends to clean every 3–4 weeks regardless, formalizing this as a contract is simply the more economical version of what they'd be doing anyway.

Contact Wagon Windows to discuss a contract rate specific to your commercial property — we quote based on glass area, frequency, and access requirements.
bullets: Contract: fixed schedule, locked pricing, no rebooking overhead — best for high-traffic retail and food service
One-off: flexible, no commitment — best for seasonal businesses or low-presentation requirements
Weekly/bi-weekly contracts receive the most favourable per-visit rates

sections.h2: Commercial Window Cleaning Costs in the Shuswap
sections.body:
Transparent pricing is a stated value for Wagon Windows, and this extends to commercial work. Here's what to expect for commercial window cleaning costs in Salmon Arm and the broader Shuswap.

Small storefronts start at $169 per clean. This covers most small retail storefronts along the Salmon Arm commercial strip — a typical one-room service business, small boutique, or professional office with a limited glass frontage.

Medium commercial properties with 6–15 panes start at $189. This covers most mid-size retail locations, restaurants with standard glass frontage, and professional offices with more significant street presence.

Larger commercial properties, multi-unit buildings, and complex configurations are quoted on-site after assessment. The variables that most affect pricing for larger commercial jobs are total glass area, interior access coordination requirements, scheduling constraints (after-hours, early morning), and frequency of service.

Recurring contract rates are negotiated directly based on frequency and volume. Weekly and bi-weekly contract clients receive locked rates that reflect the scheduling efficiency of regular routing. Monthly contract clients receive better rates than one-off clients booking the same frequency without a commitment.

For context on value: a one-off clean for a typical Salmon Arm storefront at $169–$189 represents one of the highest ROI maintenance expenditures a small business can make — the exterior impression improvement is immediate and the cost is comparable to a single hour of professional service in most trades. Businesses that invest in quarterly cleans at minimum are maintaining their property at a standard that supports both customer confidence and commercial property value.
bullets: Small storefront (up to 5 panes): from $169
Medium commercial (6–15 panes): from $189
Larger / complex commercial: quoted on-site
Recurring contract rates: contact for locked pricing
Free quotes: call (403) 837-5092

faqs.q: Can you clean our windows outside of business hours?
faqs.a: Yes — early morning before-opening cleans are our most common commercial scheduling configuration. We work from 7 AM for most commercial clients, which means glass is clean before the first customers arrive. After-hours cleans are available by arrangement. For interior cleaning in retail environments, we prefer to schedule when staff are present but before customer-facing hours, typically 7–9 AM.

faqs.q: Do you provide a completion certificate or record of service for property management or strata purposes?
faqs.a: Yes. For commercial clients who need documentation — strata corporations, property managers, lease compliance situations — we provide a dated completion record for each service visit. Contact us when booking and specify the format you need. For recurring contract clients, we can provide monthly service records automatically.

faqs.q: How do you handle commercial cleans when there are displays or merchandise behind the glass?
faqs.a: We work carefully around retail displays, signage, and merchandise visible through the glass from outside. For interior glass cleaning in retail environments, we coordinate with your staff to move merchandise from the working area temporarily and replace it after. We treat retail interiors with the same care as residential interiors — floor protection, careful squeegee handling near displays, and no product being moved without staff coordination.

faqs.q: Can you service multiple locations if we have more than one Shuswap property?
faqs.a: Yes — multi-location accounts are something we actively accommodate. If you manage two or more commercial properties in Salmon Arm or the Shuswap region, we can configure a single recurring schedule that covers all locations on a defined rotation. Multi-location accounts typically receive better per-location pricing than equivalent single-location bookings. Contact us with a list of your properties and we'll build a proposal.

faqs.q: Do you carry commercial liability insurance?
faqs.a: Yes. Wagon Windows carries general commercial liability insurance appropriate for the work we do. We're happy to provide a certificate of insurance to property managers, strata corporations, or tenants who require it as a condition of service. Request it when booking and we'll include it with your service confirmation.
